section.title: To the Church in Pergamum
section.sectionRationale: The third letter, addressed to a church living at the very center of Roman religious and political power in Asia, where Satan's throne sits. Commended for holding fast even to the martyrdom of Antipas, the church is nonetheless rebuked for tolerating teachers who counsel compromise with that surrounding culture.
section.thesis: To a church holding fast Christ's name in the shadow of Satan's throne, even to the martyrdom of Antipas, the one with the sharp, double-edged sword rebukes their tolerance of Balaam-like teaching that trades idol food and immorality for peace with the world, and calls them to repent, promising hidden manna and a white stone with a new name to the one who overcomes.
section.frameNote: Two units: Christ's self-description and his commendation of Pergamum's fidelity amid martyrdom (12-13); the rebuke of compromise, the call to repent, and the promise to the one who overcomes (14-17).

units.label: Faithful where Satan's throne sits
units.note: even to the death of Antipas

verses.text: To the angel of the church in Pergamum write: These are the words of the One who holds the sharp, double-edged sword.

I know where you live, where {{thronos_satana|the throne of Satan}} sits. Yet you have held fast to My name and have not denied your faith in Me, even in the day when My faithful witness Antipas was killed among you, where Satan dwells.

But I have a few things against you, because some of you hold to the teaching of {{balaam|Balaam}}, who taught Balak to place a stumbling block before the Israelites so they would eat food sacrificed to idols and commit {{porneia|sexual immorality}}.
In the same way, some of you also hold to the teaching of the Nicolaitans.
Therefore repent! Otherwise I will come to you shortly and wage war against them with the sword of My mouth.

He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches. To the one who overcomes, I will give the {{manna_kekrymmenon|hidden manna}}. I will also give him a {{psephos_leuke|white stone}} inscribed with a new name, known only to the one who receives it.

thronos_satana.gloss: The throne of Satan, likely evoking Pergamum's massed imperial cult temples and altars, which made the city the leading center of emperor worship and pagan religious power in Roman Asia; to confess Christ openly there was to live in especially hostile territory.

balaam.gloss: The Old Testament prophet who, unable to curse Israel directly (Numbers 22-24), counseled Balak to lure Israel into idolatry and sexual sin through the Moabite women (Numbers 25; 31:16). His name became shorthand for a teacher who leads God's people into compromise for personal gain.

porneia.gloss: Sexual immorality, often paired, as in the Old Testament prophets, with spiritual unfaithfulness through idolatry. Eating food sacrificed to idols and porneia named together the compromise of table fellowship with pagan cult and moral standards alike.

manna_kekrymmenon.gloss: The hidden manna, recalling the manna kept in a jar before the ark (Exodus 16:32-34) as a memorial of God's provision in the wilderness; promised here as future, hidden nourishment for those who refuse to compromise with the world's table.

psephos_leuke.gloss: A white stone, possibly evoking a victor's admission token, a judicial verdict of acquittal, or a guest's token of welcome to a feast. Inscribed with a new name known only to its recipient, it pictures an intimate, personal assurance of belonging to Christ.

basic.context: Pergamum was the political and religious capital of Roman Asia, thick with temples to Rome's gods and its emperors. Christ commends this church for holding fast to his name even when their own faithful witness Antipas was martyred there. But he rebukes them for tolerating teachers within their own fellowship who, like Balaam of old, counseled compromise: eating food sacrificed to idols and sexual immorality. He calls them to repent, promising hidden manna and a white stone with a new name to whoever overcomes.
basic.hermeneutics: This letter is read as addressed to a real church in a real city famous for concentrated pagan and imperial religious power. The 'teaching of Balaam' and 'teaching of the Nicolaitans' are debated as either the same compromise under two names or two related errors, but either way the point stands: tolerating false teachers who counsel compromise is itself a serious fault, even in a church that has shown real courage under persecution.
basic.originalLanguages: Christ knows they live where ho thronos tou Satana sits, the throne of Satan, likely Pergamum's imperial cult centers. He names the teaching of Balaam and the call to porneia, sexual immorality, alongside idol food. The promised reward is to manna to kekrymmenon, the hidden manna, and a psephos leuke, a white stone, with a new name known only to its receiver.
basic.typology: The hidden manna recalls the manna kept before the ark in Exodus 16:32-34, fulfilled in Christ, the true bread from heaven of John 6:31-35. Balaam's story in Numbers 22-25 stands as a lasting warning type against teachers who lead God's people into compromise. The white stone with a new name anticipates the new name promised in Isaiah 62:2 and again later in Revelation.
basic.christ: Christ presents himself here as the one with the sharp, double-edged sword from his mouth, the very image John saw in chapter 1, now aimed specifically at those teaching compromise within the church. He honors real courage under martyrdom, yet holds the whole congregation responsible for what it tolerates in its teaching, showing that faithfulness under persecution from outside does not excuse compromise tolerated from within.
basic.god: God takes seriously both fidelity under pressure and doctrinal compromise tolerated for the sake of peace with a hostile culture. The same God who commends Antipas's martyrdom warns that he will personally war against false teachers with the sword of his mouth if the church does not repent of tolerating them.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 22-25 tells Balaam's full story and its consequences for Israel. Exodus 16:32-34 records the hidden manna kept before the ark. Isaiah 62:2 promises a new name given by the Lord. Revelation 19:12 shows Christ himself bearing a name that no one knows but himself.
basic.oneStory: In the very city most saturated with pagan and imperial power, Christ's church is called not merely to survive persecution from outside but to refuse compromise from within, following ancient warnings as old as Balaam's counsel to Balak. The reward for faithfulness is intimate and personal, hidden manna and a new name known only to Christ and the one who receives it, the whole Bible's promise of God's provision and closeness pressed into a single, precious image.

context.literary: The third of seven letters, following the same sevenfold pattern, but distinguished by the sharpest concentration of specific historical and cultural detail: Satan's throne, the martyr Antipas, and named false teachings, Balaam and the Nicolaitans.
context.historical: Pergamum was the former capital of the Attalid dynasty and, under Rome, a leading center of the imperial cult, home also to a famous altar to Zeus and to the god of healing, Asclepius, whose symbol was a serpent. Confessing Christ publicly here carried unusually direct exposure to state and pagan religious power.
context.canonical: The Balaam narrative (Numbers 22-25, 31) becomes a recurring New Testament warning against teachers who trade truth for gain, echoed again in 2 Peter 2:15-16 and Jude 11, showing this letter draws on a well-established biblical type.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To honor real courage shown under persecution and martyrdom while pressing the church to see that tolerating false teachers who counsel idolatrous and immoral compromise is itself a serious failure requiring repentance, not a lesser concern beside outward faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The letter describes Pergamum's specific mix of courage and compromise; it is not a claim that every church facing persecution will necessarily also face this exact false teaching. Its abiding truth is that fidelity to Christ under pressure from outside must be matched by refusal to tolerate compromise from within.

debates.question: What was 'the throne of Satan' in Pergamum?

views.label: The concentration of imperial cult worship
views.case: Pergamum was a leading administrative and religious center for emperor worship in Roman Asia, making public refusal to honor Caesar as divine especially dangerous there, so 'Satan's throne' names this concentrated state-backed pagan power.

views.label: The cult of Asclepius and its serpent symbol
views.case: Pergamum's famous healing temple to Asclepius, whose emblem was a serpent-entwined staff drawing pilgrims from across the region, may be specifically in view, evoking the ancient serpent, Satan, directly rather than only imperial politics.

debates.bearing: Whichever specific cult is chiefly meant, both readings agree Pergamum concentrated pagan religious and political power in a way that made public faithfulness to Christ unusually costly, explaining both Antipas's martyrdom and the pressure toward compromise the letter goes on to rebuke.

debates.question: Are 'the teaching of Balaam' and 'the teaching of the Nicolaitans' in verses 14-15 the same error or two distinct errors?

views.label: One and the same compromise under two names
views.case: Balaam's Old Testament strategy of luring Israel into idol food and sexual immorality (Numbers 25, 31:16) is read as simply the historical type the contemporary Nicolaitans were repeating in Pergamum, so 'teaching of Balaam' functions almost as a nickname for the Nicolaitans' actual practice.

views.label: Two related but distinct teachings
views.case: Others read them as two identifiable groups or strands of teaching present together in Pergamum's churches, both leading in the same direction of accommodation with the surrounding pagan culture, but not strictly identical.

debates.bearing: Either way, Christ treats the church's doctrinal tolerance of such teachers as a serious charge against the whole congregation, not merely against the individual teachers themselves, calling the church corporately to repent.

study.originalLanguages: Christ knows Pergamum lives where ho thronos tou Satana sits, the throne of Satan, likely the city's massed imperial and pagan cult centers. Within the church itself, some hold the teaching of Balaam, tied to porneia, sexual immorality, and idol food, alongside the teaching of the Nicolaitans. The promised reward for the one who overcomes is intimate and hidden: to manna to kekrymmenon, the hidden manna, and a psephos leuke, a white stone, bearing a new name known only to its recipient.

typology.type: The hidden manna kept before the ark, Exodus 16:32-34
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the true bread from heaven, John 6:31-35, and promised here as future, hidden nourishment for the faithful.
A lasting warning type against teachers who lead God's people into idolatrous and immoral compromise, echoed again in 2 Peter 2:15-16 and Jude 11.

typology.type: The new name promised by the Lord, Isaiah 62:2
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in the white stone's new name here, and again in Christ's own unknown name in Revelation 19:12.

study.christ: Christ addresses Pergamum as the one with the sharp, double-edged sword from his mouth, the very image John saw in the opening vision, now turned specifically against those teaching compromise within the church. He honors Antipas's martyrdom by name, calling him 'my faithful witness,' yet warns that he will personally war against false teachers with that same sword if the church does not repent of tolerating them, showing that his commendation of courage under persecution never excuses complacency toward compromise from within.
study.god: God is shown honoring costly faithfulness by name, remembering Antipas as his faithful witness even in death, while just as directly confronting doctrinal compromise tolerated inside the church for the sake of an easier peace with the surrounding culture. His reward for overcoming is deeply personal, hidden manna and a new name known only between himself and the believer, showing that intimacy with God, not mere survival, is the goal of faithfulness.

crossReferences.note: Balaam's full story: unable to curse Israel directly, he counsels Balak to lure them into idolatry and immorality instead.
The hidden manna kept before the ark as a memorial of God's provision in the wilderness.
The Lord promises his people a new name, echoed in the white stone's inscribed new name.
Christ himself is shown bearing a name that no one knows but he himself.

study.oneStory: In the city most saturated with pagan and imperial power, a church that had already paid in blood for its public faithfulness is nonetheless called to refuse the quieter, more comfortable compromise of tolerating false teachers within its own walls. Christ's reward for overcoming is as old as the wilderness and as personal as a name only he and the believer will ever know, the whole Bible's story of provision and intimacy with God pressed into two small, precious images: hidden manna and a white stone.

sermon.title: Pergamum, Church that Compromised
sermon.why: Ferguson works directly through Revelation 2:12-17, drawing out how a church can show real courage under persecution from without while still tolerating serious doctrinal compromise from within, and what repentance, the hidden manna, and the white stone mean for such a church.

quiz.q: What does Christ commend the church in Pergamum for?

options.text: Holding fast his name and not denying their faith, even when Antipas was martyred among them.
options.note: Verse 13 gives this exact commendation.

options.text: Their complete rejection of every form of false teaching.
options.note: The letter goes on to rebuke them for tolerating false teaching; the commendation is specifically about fidelity under persecution.

options.text: Their success in converting the city's pagan temples.
options.note: The commendation concerns steadfast confession under pressure, not a claim about converting the city.

options.text: Nothing; the letter contains only rebuke.
options.note: Verse 13 gives real, specific commendation before the complaint.

quiz.q: What does 'the teaching of Balaam' represent in this letter?

options.text: Counsel to compromise with pagan culture through idol food and sexual immorality.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly ties Balaam's teaching to eating food sacrificed to idols and sexual immorality.

options.text: A call to complete separation from all non-believers.
options.note: The teaching is toward compromise with pagan practice, the opposite of separation.

options.text: A demand for stricter observance of the law.
options.note: Balaam's counsel led toward compromise and sin, not toward stricter faithfulness.

options.text: It has no specific content mentioned in the letter.
options.note: Verse 14 names the specific content of this teaching directly.

quiz.q: What does Christ call the church to do about the teaching of Balaam and the Nicolaitans?

options.text: Repent, or he will come and war against them with the sword of his mouth.
options.note: Verse 16 gives this exact call and warning.

options.text: Simply ignore the teachers and hope they leave on their own.
options.note: The letter calls for active repentance, not passive waiting.

options.text: Join the teaching in order to keep peace within the congregation.
options.note: The letter calls the church to repent of tolerating this teaching, not to join it.

options.text: Nothing is required of the church regarding this teaching.
options.note: A direct call to repent is issued in verse 16.

quiz.q: What is promised to the one who overcomes in this letter?

options.text: The hidden manna and a white stone with a new name known only to the receiver.
options.note: Verse 17 gives this exact twofold promise.

options.text: Political rule over the city of Pergamum.
options.note: The promise is spiritual and intimate, not earthly political power.

options.text: Immediate exemption from all future persecution.
options.note: The promise concerns hidden manna and a new name, not a guarantee against future hardship.

options.text: No promise is given, only warning.
options.note: A specific, twofold reward closes the letter.

quiz.q: What does the phrase 'where Satan's throne sits' most likely describe?

options.text: Pergamum's concentration of imperial cult worship and pagan religious power.
options.note: This is the leading historical understanding of the phrase, given Pergamum's role as a center of emperor worship.

options.text: A literal throne visible to John in his vision.
options.note: It describes the city's spiritual and political character, not a literal object John saw.

options.text: A reference to the church's own leadership structure.
options.note: It describes the city itself as a center of hostile pagan power, not the church's internal leadership.

options.text: It has no connection to Pergamum's actual historical situation.
options.note: It is closely tied to Pergamum's well-documented role as a leading imperial cult center.

questions: Pergamum showed real courage under persecution yet tolerated compromise within. Where might you be strong in one area of faithfulness while quietly tolerating compromise in another?
Christ names Antipas specifically as 'my faithful witness.' How does it encourage you to know that Christ remembers costly faithfulness by name, even when no one else may?
The teaching of Balaam offered an easier path: compromise a little, and avoid conflict with the surrounding culture. Where are you tempted toward that same kind of quiet compromise?
The promise to the overcomer is deeply personal, a new name known only between Christ and the believer. What does that kind of intimate, hidden reward mean to you, compared to public recognition?
Christ calls the whole church to repent of what some among them were teaching. How do you take responsibility, not just for your own faithfulness, but for what your community tolerates?

translationNotes.note: 'Antipas was killed among you' preserves almost no independent historical detail beyond this verse; early church tradition later expanded his story, but the text itself simply honors him as Christ's faithful witness.

'Food sacrificed to idols' refers to meat from pagan temple sacrifices, often served at trade guild banquets; eating it could signal participation in the guild's patron god's worship, the compromise Balaam's teaching is said to have encouraged.

'A white stone' has been read as a courtroom acquittal token, a festival admission ticket, or a victor's prize marker; the text itself leaves the precise image open, focusing instead on the personal, hidden new name it bears.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the project's makor-scripture toolkit for Revelation 2:12-17. No em or en dashes appeared in the source verses for this section. Sermon link for Sinclair Ferguson's 'Pergamum, Church that Compromised' verified live this session on thegospelcoalition.org, covering this exact passage.
